metadata.title: Exclusive Inclusion: Governance Architecture and Systematic Identity-Based Exclusion
metadata.author: Seth C. Oranburg
metadata.institution: Catholic University of America, Columbus School of Law
metadata.core_thesis: American universities produce systematic identity-based exclusion through governance architecture, not despite their commitments to pluralism, but because governance structures make those commitments unenforceable.

core_concept.name: Exclusive Inclusion
core_concept.definition: The governance state in which an institution claims to protect all members while structurally ensuring that some are unprotected because self-perpetuating boards, hollow fiduciary duties, and the absence of stakeholder standing create an accountability void that organized factions can exploit.
core_concept.key_distinction: The disease is the governance void. The exclusion is the symptom.

distinguishing_from.individual_prejudice: Not about bad actors following institutional policy
distinguishing_from.disparate_impact: Not about facially neutral policies with discriminatory effects
distinguishing_from.organizational_hypocrisy: Not about saying one thing and doing another intentionally
distinguishing_from.institutional_isomorphism: Not just explaining why orgs adopt similar structures

evidence.survey_data: 57.3% reported direct antisemitism; 63.4% reported bystander exposure
evidence.institutional_failure: DEI offices reclassified antisemitism complaints as political expression
evidence.scale: UCLA targeting incidents up 3,150 (4,130 total); UC Berkeley up 531

identity_groups_affected.mechanism: Faculty for Justice in Palestine used departmental authority over hiring, curriculum, and programming to advance boycotts excluding Israeli scholars by nationality
identity_groups_affected.governance_problem: Board had no mechanism to intervene; departments captured governance structures

evidence.study: Four public universities recognized as DEI leaders in computer science
evidence.finding: Gender equity programs systematically avoided race

identity_groups_affected.mechanism: Institutional DEI roles narrowly defined diversity; protected gender language while excluding Women of Color
identity_groups_affected.governance_problem: DEI infrastructure functioned as structural filter, leaving racialized power dynamics undisturbed

identity_groups_affected.group: Black Students at Liberal Arts Colleges

evidence.study: 2024 Socius study of one institution with explicit DEI commitments
evidence.finding: Facially neutral behavioral policy produced systematic racial inequality through differential enforcement

identity_groups_affected.governance_problem: Governance structures at departmental level lacked accountability; board reasoning from reputational frames foreclosed equitable remedies

evidence.fire_survey: 2024 survey of 6,269 faculty at 55 four-year institutions
evidence.self_censorship: 47% of conservatives self-censor vs. 19% of liberals
evidence.written_work: 35% tone down written work from fear of controversy (higher than McCarthyism era)

identity_groups_affected.mechanism: Hiring committees use vague 'fit' criteria as ideological gatekeeping device
identity_groups_affected.governance_problem: Disaggregated departmental hiring with no accountability for ideological screening

identity_groups_affected.legal_framework: ADA and Section 504 create legal entitlements
identity_groups_affected.problem: Principal-agent breakdown: compliance officers write policies, operational units decide enforcement
identity_groups_affected.outcome: Legal entitlements converted to contingent privileges
identity_groups_affected.governance_problem: No internal accountability mechanism bridges discretionary enforcement gap

disentanglement_matrix.description: Diagnostic tool for identifying exclusive inclusion by separating protected individual expression from institutional identity-based exclusion

axis_1_actor.institutional: Organization deploying authority, resources, or official channels

axis_2_target.identity: People defined by ethnicity, national origin, religious affiliation, disability status

individual_perspective.name: Protected Expression
individual_perspective.example: Professor declines collaboration over foreign policy disagreement

individual_identity.name: Personal Discrimination
individual_identity.example: Professor refuses to supervise students of particular religion
individual_identity.legal_status: Prohibited by Title VI, enforcement gap only

institutional_perspective.name: Governance Question
institutional_perspective.example: University divests through board channels or faculty senate passes resolution
institutional_perspective.legal_status: Sound in institutional neutrality (Kalven principle), not civil rights

institutional_identity.name: Exclusive Inclusion
institutional_identity.example: Department uses authority to systematically exclude scholars by national origin or religion

jurisdictional_retreat.definition: Actors reframe acts of exclusive inclusion (bottom-right) as protected individual expression (top-left)
jurisdictional_retreat.mechanism: Institutional character disappears; identity-based effect disappears; act reframed as protected individual speech
jurisdictional_retreat.case_example: Academic boycott: Faculty for Justice in Palestine chapters used departmental authority to exclude Israeli scholars, then claimed individual academic freedom when challenged
jurisdictional_retreat.matrix_function: Exposes the retreat by forcing threshold question: Did the institution take this act through institutional channels using institutional authority?

disentanglement_matrix.operational_test: Disproportionate impact, not perfect sorting. When institutional action excludes 90% of nationals from particular country, identity-based character predominates. Consistent with Title VI disparate impact doctrine.

governance_void.definition: Institutions that wield public influence, receive public subsidy, perform public functions, while governing themselves as private corporations answerable to no external constituency

three_architectural_features.description: Every colonial-era university charter vests ultimate governance authority in a board that selects its own members
three_architectural_features.example: Harvard Corporation governed by internal appointment since 1650; Yale trustees elect replacements
three_architectural_features.problem: Faculty, students, alumni, public have no binding governance role; institution can absorb external criticism indefinitely
three_architectural_features.scholarly_anchor: Standard nonprofit governance literature; state nonprofit corporation acts

three_architectural_features.description: Nonprofit law nominally requires trustees to act within stated mission, but when trustees write mission statements and statements are vague, constraint is formal not operative
three_architectural_features.example: Harvard mission: 'educate future leaders and strive toward a more just world' is not judicially enforceable
three_architectural_features.problem: Courts defer under business judgment rule; vague mission preserves maximum flexibility and avoids judicial review
three_architectural_features.scholarly_anchor: Alan Palmiter—'The Forgotten Fiduciary Obligation'; Johnny Rex Buckles CUA L. Rev. 62(4):913 (2013)

three_architectural_features.description: Students and faculty cannot bring derivative suits to enforce fiduciary duties; only state AGs have nominal standing
three_architectural_features.problem: State AGs chronically under-resourced and politically disinclined to sue wealthy institutions; result is structural impunity
three_architectural_features.scholarly_anchor: Johnny Rex Buckles; Turner v. Victoria, Cal. 2023 (expanding standing to internal stakeholders)

governance_void.consequence: Disaggregated governance produces aggregate pattern—multiple departments, committees, units lack accountability; central administration claims commitment to pluralism while individual units claim legitimate academic judgment; excluded group has no recourse

why_existing_law_misses_it.remedies_imposed: Definitions of antisemitism, liaisons, climate surveys
why_existing_law_misses_it.what_it_missed: Zero governance reform; no stakeholder standing created; governance void persists when enforcement ends
why_existing_law_misses_it.case: President and Fellows of Harvard College v. U.S. Dept. of Health & Human Services (D. Mass. 2025)

why_existing_law_misses_it.what_it_misses: Institutional action through official channels and resources; identity-based effects; institutional accountability framework
why_existing_law_misses_it.problem: Actors retreat behind academic freedom; institutional character and identity-based effect disappear

why_existing_law_misses_it.what_fails: Principal-agent breakdown; discretionary enforcement converts entitlements to contingent privileges
why_existing_law_misses_it.problem: Statute cannot force structural compliance; statute lacks internal accountability mechanism

why_existing_law_misses_it.summary: Federal coercion addresses symptoms. Compliance addresses optics. Neither reaches the governance void.

second_order_diversity.theoretical_framework: Heather Gerken work on disaggregated institutional design
second_order_diversity.how_it_works: Multiple departments, committees, governance units can institutionalize exclusion through aggregation rules, control-influence tradeoffs, cycling effects
second_order_diversity.key_insight: Formal pluralist commitments at institutional level do not guarantee plural outcomes when subunit governance structures lack accountability
second_order_diversity.mechanism: Each subunit adopts policies appearing reasonable in isolation, but aggregate to systematic exclusion of particular groups; no single decision-maker responsible for pattern

three_structural_reforms.mechanism: Legislatures permit currently enrolled students and currently employed faculty to bring derivative actions enforcing existing fiduciary duties against nonprofit educational boards
safeguards: Heightened pleading standards (Fed. R. Civ. P. 23.1 analogs)
Judicial review for abuse of discretion preserving board deference
three_structural_reforms.primary_effect: Deterrent—boards exercise greater care knowing stakeholders can enforce duties
three_structural_reforms.assessment: Most legally grounded; deterrent effect may be sufficient alone

three_structural_reforms.mechanism: State legislatures require tax-exempt educational institutions to include specific, measurable commitments to equal treatment in mission statements as condition of charitable registration
three_structural_reforms.how_it_works: Converts hollow duty of obedience into justiciable standard
three_structural_reforms.case_study: Robertson family donated $35M to Woodrow Wilson School; six-year litigation over diverted endowment; settlement before final judgment showed courts can evaluate institutional conduct when mission is specific enough
three_structural_reforms.settlement_outcome: $50M to new independent foundation; total recovery ~$90M including legal fees
three_structural_reforms.assessment: Needs doctrinal stretch but Robertson precedent shows it can work

three_structural_reforms.model: Hospital community benefit standard (Rev. Rul. 69-545; I.R.C. §501(r))
three_structural_reforms.mechanism: IRS conditions 501(c)(3) tax exemption on demonstrated governance structures preventing systematic identity-based exclusion
three_structural_reforms.rationale: Universities receive ~$37B annually in tax exemptions + federal research funding but face no comparable accountability requirement as hospitals
three_structural_reforms.unconstitutional_conditions_response: Proposed test targets governance structures not viewpoint/speech; Rumsfeld v. FAIR (military recruiter equal access) established that conduct conditions don't violate First Amendment
three_structural_reforms.assessment: Most powerful leverage but most politically fragile and administratively reversible

three_structural_reforms.mutual_reinforcement: Standing without enforceable standards is empty; standards without standing are unenforceable; external accountability without internal mechanisms can be satisfied on paper. Together they create overlapping accountability redundancy.

open_questions_for_workshop.title: Where does individual become institutional?
open_questions_for_workshop.easy_case: Department deploys budget and official authority for boycott = institutional
open_questions_for_workshop.hard_case: Ten department chairs circulate letter on departmental letterhead; faculty senate votes on resolution. Does collective faculty action through shared governance channels become institutional action?
open_questions_for_workshop.presenter_uncertainty: Is key question whether institution lent authority/resources, or just whether official channels used? Formulation may be too permissive or too restrictive. Answer may chill legitimate faculty governance.

open_questions_for_workshop.title: Have I earned the cross-identity claim?
open_questions_for_workshop.concern: Oct. 7 opening may make paper read as fundamentally about Jewish students, with four other groups as supporting evidence (illustrative vs. structural claim)
analytical_questions: Is any identity group analytically thin?
Conservative faculty worries me most—is viewpoint exclusion same governance mechanism as identity exclusion?
If mechanism is structurally identical, why does existing law treat them so differently?
Does legal asymmetry prove structural point or undermine cross-identity analogy?

open_questions_for_workshop.title: Which reform is worth fighting for?
open_questions_for_workshop.context: Three reforms mutually reinforcing but political landscape may allow only one

reform_assessments.stakeholder_standing: Most legally grounded; deterrent effect may be sufficient; decades of corporate derivative suit experience support feasibility
reform_assessments.mission_specificity: Needs doctrinal stretch; Robertson precedent shows principle is sound; requires legislative mandate of specificity
reform_assessments.operational_test_reform: Most powerful federal leverage over all tax-exempt institutions simultaneously; most fragile politically; easily reversed by administration

primary_sources.author: Johnny Rex Buckles
primary_sources.title: How Deep are the Springs of Obedience Norms that Bind the Overseers of Charities?
primary_sources.relevance: Comprehensive taxonomy of obedience norms; establishes that duty of obedience is forgotten fiduciary obligation rendered toothless by modern practice

primary_sources.author: Alan Palmiter
primary_sources.title: The Duty of Obedience: The Forgotten Duty
primary_sources.relevance: Articulates how duty of obedience has been rendered toothless; legal and historical analysis

primary_sources.author: Heather K. Gerken
primary_sources.title: Second-Order Diversity and Institutional Design
primary_sources.relevance: Explains how disaggregated governance structures institutionalize exclusion; aggregation rules and control-influence tradeoffs

primary_sources.author: Mark Suchman
primary_sources.title: Managing Legitimacy: Strategic and Institutional Approaches
primary_sources.relevance: Organizational theory: distinction between perception of legitimacy and structural conditions sustaining it

primary_sources.author: Henry Hansmann
primary_sources.title: The Evolving Law of Nonprofit Organizations
primary_sources.relevance: Nonprofit governance theory; voice and exit as stakeholder discipline mechanisms

primary_sources.author: Elinor Ostrom
primary_sources.title: Governing the Commons
primary_sources.relevance: Commons governance: institutions excluding affected parties from decision-making tend to serve insiders at expense of outsiders

primary_sources.author: Stokely Carmichael & Charles V. Hamilton
primary_sources.title: Black Power (1967)
primary_sources.relevance: Foundational distinction between individual prejudice and established forces generating inequality without requiring individual hostility

empirical_research.finding: 57.3% of Jewish students reported direct antisemitism; 63.4% bystander exposure
empirical_research.institutions: UCLA (up 3,150 incidents, 4,130 total), UC Berkeley (up 531), UC Santa Cruz

empirical_research.finding: Four DEI-leading CS departments systematically avoided race in gender equity programs
empirical_research.mechanism: Institutional DEI roles narrowly defined diversity; protected gender language while excluding Women of Color

empirical_research.finding: Facially neutral behavioral policy produced systematic racial inequality through differential enforcement

empirical_research.study: FIRE 2024 survey of 6,269 faculty at 55 institutions
empirical_research.finding: 47% of conservatives self-censor vs. 19% of liberals; 35% tone down written work from fear (higher than McCarthyism era)
Principal-agent breakdown converts legal entitlements to contingent privileges; no internal accountability mechanism

key_cases.case_name: President and Fellows of Harvard College v. U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
key_cases.holding: District court found administration used antisemitism as smokescreen for ideologically-motivated assault on premier universities
key_cases.relevance: Even when administration wins, governance void persists; nine-figure settlements produce zero governance reform

Model for mission specificity reform; $35M donation in 1961; six-year litigation over diverted endowment; settlement before final judgment; $50M to new foundation; shows courts can evaluate institutional conduct when mission is specific
Expanded standing to internal stakeholders; supports legislative authority to create enforcement mechanisms for charitable entities

key_cases.holding: Conditioning federal funding on equal access for military recruiters did not violate universities' First Amendment rights because condition regulates conduct, not speech
key_cases.relevance: Supports operational test reform: IRS conditioning tax exemption on governance structures does not violate First Amendment
Disparate impact doctrine; relevant to disentanglement matrix's operational test (disproportionate impact suffices)

key_cases.case_name: Guardians Association v. Civil Service Commission
key_cases.relevance: Title VI disparate impact doctrine; supports operational test of disproportionate impact

slides.note: Work in progress; designed for 20-minute presentation
